metadata.title: Who was Albert Einstein?
metadata.color_semantics: CREAM #F0EAD6 ground; INK #000000 all text; TEAL #009E73 true/confirmed; CRIMSON #D55E00 quantum/surprising; SLATE #4D4D4D muted structure
metadata.description_blurb: In 1905, a 26-year-old patent clerk in Bern sent five papers that remade physics. Light is not a wave — it is a particle. Mass and energy are the same substance. Space and time bend. Albert Einstein didn't just extend what came before; he saw what was actually there. This is the story of that year, and the man who couldn't stop at it.

beats.narration_text: In 1900, Max Planck invented a mathematical trick to fix a broken equation. He quantized energy — and then immediately tried to walk it back. Five years later, a patent clerk in Bern read Planck's trick and asked: what if it isn't a trick at all? What if it's actually true? That question, taken seriously in the spring of 1905, changed everything.

There was a puzzle that had no satisfying explanation. Shine light on a metal surface — any metal — and electrons fly out. But the amount of light doesn't matter. What matters is its frequency. Dim blue light ejects electrons. Brilliant red light does nothing. This is wrong, by every intuition classical physics had built. A wave should deposit energy continuously; more light should mean more energy and more electrons. Instead: nothing, nothing, nothing — then suddenly, yes, above a threshold.

card.copy: Why does light eject electrons from metal — but only above a certain frequency, regardless of intensity?
card.sub: The Photoelectric Effect — still unexplained in 1904

beats.narration_text: Einstein's answer was simple in the way that genuinely right answers are. Light doesn't come in a wave — it comes in packets. Photons. Each photon carries energy E equals h times f: Planck's constant multiplied by frequency. Below a threshold frequency, no single photon has enough energy to knock an electron loose — no matter how many arrive. Above it, one photon and one electron make a pair. Intensity just means more photons; frequency means more energy per photon. The threshold is a floor, not a dial.

production_viz.label: E=hf equation on medhavy cream (#F0EAD6); frequency ladder with CRIMSON (#D55E00) above threshold, SLATE (#4D4D4D) below; electron ejection arrow in TEAL (#009E73)

beats.narration_text: The interesting part is what this committed him to. If light comes in discrete packets, then energy is not continuous — it is quantized. Planck had said something similar, but only as a bookkeeping trick for blackbody radiation. Einstein said: no, this is real. Light is actually granular. The experiment doesn't just fit; it only makes sense this way.

card.copy: Light is not a continuous wave. It arrives as discrete packets — photons. Energy = h × f.
card.sub: Einstein, March 1905 — On a Heuristic Point of View Concerning the Production and Transformation of Light

beats.narration_text: Three months later, the same office, the same year. Einstein published special relativity — and then a short follow-up that read almost like an afterthought: does the inertia of a body depend upon its energy content? The answer was yes. E equals m c squared. Mass and energy are the same substance, viewed from different angles. The speed of light squared — roughly ninety quadrillion meters squared per second squared — is the exchange rate. A microgram of matter contains the energy of a city block of explosives.

production_viz.label: E=mc² equation on medhavy cream; bar chart: 1 kg mass (SLATE, small) vs 9×10^16 J energy (CRIMSON, tall); ×c² conversion arrow in TEAL

beats.narration_text: Einstein won the Nobel Prize in 1921 — for the photoelectric effect, not for relativity. The Nobel committee found relativity too speculative. Meanwhile, by 1926, quantum mechanics had grown into a full theory under Bohr, Heisenberg, and Schrödinger. Einstein hated it. The universe was fundamentally random, said Bohr. The Copenhagen interpretation made probability intrinsic, not just a matter of incomplete knowledge. "God does not play dice with the universe," Einstein wrote. He was wrong. The man who unlocked the quantum world spent the rest of his career trying to close the door.

card.copy: "God does not play dice with the universe."
card.sub: — Albert Einstein, in a letter to Max Born, December 1926

beats.act: THE MIRACLE YEAR AND WHAT FOLLOWED
beats.narration_text: From five papers in one year, an entire century of physics unfolds. Atomic theory. Nuclear energy. GPS — which requires relativistic corrections to work. The laser. The transistor. Quantum cryptography. And ten years after the miracle year, in 1915, general relativity: gravity is not a force but the curvature of spacetime. Black holes. The expanding universe. The Big Bang. All of it traces back to a clerk who decided Planck's formula was telling the truth about reality.

artifactLines: March 1905 — Photoelectric effect: light is quantized (Nobel Prize 1921)
May 1905 — Brownian motion: atoms are real and measurable
June 1905 — Special relativity: c is constant; space and time are relative
1915 — General relativity: gravity is curved spacetime
1935 — EPR paradox: Einstein's challenge to quantum completeness

beats.narration_text: Einstein was born in Ulm, Germany, in 1879. He became a Swiss citizen and took a post at the Bern patent office in 1902. The miracle year happened while he was there. In 1933, when the Nazi government came to power, he was in the United States on a lecture tour. He didn't go back. The Institute for Advanced Study at Princeton gave him an office. He became an American citizen in 1940. He spent his last twenty years on a unified field theory — gravity and electromagnetism in one framework — and never finished it. He died in 1955 and asked to be cremated. There is no grave.

card.copy: Albert Einstein · 1879–1955 · Patent clerk, then professor, then refugee, then citizen. Nobel 1921. Princeton 1933. Unified field theory: unfinished.
card.sub: He wanted to understand how the universe worked. He got most of the way there.

beats.narration_text: Medhavy — for curious minds. Subscribe and explore more.
